summary: In this 1995 interview, Steve Jobs reflects on his early experiences with computers, the founding of Apple, and his eventual ouster. He shares his philosophies on product development, the importance of craftsmanship over mere ideas, the danger of sales-driven corporate monopolies, and the necessity of hiring 'A-players.'

principles.statement: Computer science is a liberal art that teaches you how to think.
principles.rationale: Learning to program acts as a mirror to your thought process, much like going to law school, teaching you how to think in a structured, rigorous way.
principles.source_quote: I think everybody in this country should learn how to program a computer should learn a computer language because it teaches you how to think.

principles.statement: Great products come from deep understanding of content, not from institutionalized process.
principles.rationale: As companies grow, they try to replicate success by institutionalizing process, but they eventually confuse the process with the content itself, forgetting how to actually make great products.
principles.source_quote: people get very confused that the process is the content... it's not process it's content.

principles.rationale: In software, the dynamic range between average and best is 50 to 1 or 100 to 1. When you gather enough A-players, they love working together and become self-policing because they refuse to work with B and C players.
principles.source_quote: I've built a lot of My Success off finding these truly gifted people and not settling for B and C players but really going for the a players

principles.statement: Direct, honest feedback is essential for top performers.
principles.rationale: Really good people know they are good, so you don't need to baby their egos. The most important thing is the work, and you must clearly point out when their work isn't good enough to support the team's goal.
principles.source_quote: you don't have to Baby people's egos so much and what really matters is the work... point out to them when um they're not their work isn't good enough

mental_models.name: The Rock Tumbler
mental_models.description: A metaphor for how a passionate, talented team develops a great product. Just as rough stones in a tumbler create friction and noise but eventually emerge as beautiful polished rocks, a team argues, fights, and bumps up against each other to polish each other and their ideas.
mental_models.example: Jobs uses this metaphor to describe the intense, collaborative, and sometimes argumentative dynamic of the original Macintosh team.

mental_models.name: Monopoly Rot
mental_models.description: A pattern where a technology company achieves a monopoly, causing product improvements to no longer drive revenue. This shifts power to sales and marketing, driving product people out of decision-making and causing the company to forget how to make great products.
mental_models.example: Jobs cites IBM and Xerox as examples of companies where 'toner heads' (sales/marketing) took over, leading to a loss of product sensibility and craftsmanship.

heuristics: Always ask why you do things; don't accept 'that's just the way it's done' as an answer.
Don't care about being right; care about success and doing the right thing.

signature_quotes.text: I think everybody in this country should learn how to program a computer should learn a computer language because it teaches you how to think.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the value of his early experiences writing programs.

signature_quotes.text: There's a just a tremendous amount of craftsmanship in in between a great idea and a great product.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the 'disease' of thinking that an idea is 90% of the work.

signature_quotes.text: I don't mind being wrong and I'll admit that I'm wrong a lot it doesn't really matter to me too much what matters to me is that we do the right thing.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing his management style and willingness to change his mind when presented with evidence.

signature_quotes.text: The only problem with Microsoft is they just have no taste. They have absolutely no taste.
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing Microsoft's success and their lack of original ideas or cultural infusion in their products.

anti_patterns.description: The disease of thinking that a really great idea is 90% of the work.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the tremendous amount of craftsmanship, daily problem-solving, and trade-offs required to turn an idea into a tangible product. Ideas change and grow as you encounter the realities of engineering and manufacturing.

anti_patterns.description: Letting sales and marketing run a product company.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Sales and marketing executives often have no conception of a good product versus a bad product, lack the craftsmanship required to build one, and have no real feeling in their hearts about wanting to help the customer.

Companies try to institutionalize the magic of their initial success through management processes, eventually forgetting that deep understanding of the actual content (the product) is what drives success.

anti_patterns.description: Settling for B and C players.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: In software, the difference between average and best is massive (50 to 1). B and C players drag down the quality of the work, and true A-players will not want to work with them.
